questions.question: Why does avoidance feel good in the moment but harmful in the long run?

options.text: It provides immediate relief, but each avoidance teaches your brain the situation was truly dangerous, making anxiety stronger over time
It is only harmful if you avoid the same situation more than three times
It feels good but is harmful because it makes you appear weak to others
Avoidance is always harmful — there is no short-term benefit

questions.explanation: The relief from avoidance is the reward that teaches your brain 'avoidance equals safety.' Every time you avoid, you send a message to your amygdala that the situation was truly dangerous. This makes the alarm more likely to fire next time, strengthening anxiety with each cycle.

questions.question: What does the Shrinking Room metaphor describe?

options.text: How anxiety makes your physical environment feel claustrophobic
How each avoided situation builds another wall, making your world progressively smaller over time
How anxiety shrinks the amygdala when left untreated
How social withdrawal reduces the number of triggering situations you encounter

questions.explanation: The Shrinking Room metaphor illustrates how avoidance works over time. Each avoided situation is like building a new wall — it feels safer in the moment, but your world gradually gets smaller and smaller until life becomes about managing fear rather than living fully.

questions.question: According to the lesson, what are the long-term costs of avoidance?

options.text: Only physical symptoms — it has no emotional costs
It can take away confidence, opportunities, and joy while reinforcing the belief that you cannot cope
It makes you dependent on others for support
Its only cost is time wasted on worry

questions.explanation: Avoidance has three major long-term costs: it erodes your confidence by reinforcing the belief 'I cannot cope,' it takes away opportunities like jobs and relationships, and it shrinks your world — removing joy and freedom until life revolves around managing fear.

questions.question: What is the goal of facing your fears through gentle approaching, according to the lesson?

options.text: To prove that feared situations are not dangerous through logic
To learn that you can feel anxious and still do the things that matter to you
To condition yourself to enjoy previously feared situations

questions.explanation: The goal of exposure is not to become fearless. It is to learn that you can feel anxious and still take action — that you are more capable than your anxiety has led you to believe. Bravery is not the absence of fear; it is action in the presence of fear.

questions.question: Take a moment to think honestly about one thing you have been avoiding because of anxiety. What is it, and what would it mean for your life if you could do that thing freely again?
questions.explanation: Naming the specific thing you have been avoiding — and connecting it to something you genuinely want — is the first real step toward change. The meaning behind the goal is what will sustain you through the discomfort of facing it gradually.
